title: 48" Turned Leg Vanity
summary: An open-back bathroom vanity built around purchased turned legs, with a notched plywood shelf and pocket-holed face frames sized to your sink.
description:
Purchased turned legs are the design's whole visual identity, and they're also why this plan can't give you one fixed cut list — every leg style has a slightly different stretcher-block height and profile, so every board here gets cut to fit the actual legs and vanity top you buy, not a number off this page. Buy your legs, sink, and top first, then measure from those before cutting anything.

The back is left open by design, for easier access to plumbing — a solid plywood back is mentioned as an option, but the source's own build skips it on purpose. The bottom shelf is notched around the turned legs rather than boxed between them, which is what lets it slide fully back against the wall instead of stopping at the first obstruction.

tools.note: Notches the bottom shelf around the turned legs.

materials.name: Turned vanity legs, with bottom stretcher block
materials.note: Buy first — every other measurement is taken from these.

cutList.note: Cut to fit your actual legs.
Cut to fit your legs and vanity top.
Cut to fit distance between the legs' stretcher blocks.
Notched to fit around the turned legs.
Optional; sized to your storage layout.

steps.title: Build the leg-end assembly
steps.body: Build the two leg sets flush to the insides — one side sits against the wall, the other holds hardware such as toilet paper mounts. Building flush this way also makes mounting drawer slides easier later. Use 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket hole screws throughout.
materials: Turned vanity legs, with bottom stretcher block

steps.title: Build and attach the face frames
steps.body: Build two identical face frames for the front and back using 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket hole screws, then attach to the two leg-end assemblies. Build these to suit your specific sink size and location — measure your sink and vanity top before cutting, and leave generous clearance around the sink opening.

steps.title: Add the bottom shelf trim
steps.body: Finish the trim for the bottom shelf, matching it to the legs' stretcher-block height. If pocket holes won't bite well into your turned legs, place them on the outside face instead — fine for a painted finish, though a stained finish may call for insetting the trim slightly to hide this.

steps.title: Notch and attach the bottom shelf
steps.body: Cut the bottom shelf panel to a plain rectangle first, then notch out the corners with a jigsaw to fit around the turned legs. Drill 3/4" pocket holes around the underside and attach to the shelf trim with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws. For a long vanity or a heavy top, a hidden center support under the shelf adds extra strength.

steps.title: Add interior partitions
steps.body: Add partitions to separate interior storage and support the center of the shelf above, sized to your own layout. Attach with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket hole screws.

steps.title: Finish
steps.body: Sand every surface and finish. Add doors, drawers, or pullouts to fit the openings as desired before installing your sink and vanity top.

images.alt: 48 inch bathroom vanity with turned legs
